paper_title: Tackling Discrepancies in Trade Data: The Harvard Growth Lab International Trade Datasets
research_answer: The gains from improved methodology are substantial. Standard Comtrade HS1992 datasets capture around 6 million positive trade flows (at the origin-destination-product level) in recent years. Using mirroring alone — inferring a country's trade from what its partners report — recovers approximately 2 million additional data points per year beyond that. A comprehensive methodology combining mirroring with improved classification concordance reaches around 9 million positive trade flows, representing a 50% increase in data coverage over standard Comtrade. To put this in perspective, even 9 million flows represents only about 3% of the theoretical maximum of ~275 million observations if all countries traded all products with all partners — illustrating that trade data, even at its best, captures a sparse slice of all possible trade relationships. The recovered data for non-reporting countries has been validated against IMF Balance of Payments statistics and shows high correlation.
supporting_quotes: In recent years, our methodology results in around 9 million observations. [...] our dataset, recovers the most data points, identifying up to 9M positive trade flows compared to only around 6M flows captured by Comtrade's standard HS1992 datasets. This represents a 50% increase in data coverage through our approach.
The mirroring-only approach (orange line) provides an intermediate gain, recovering approximately 2M additional data points beyond the Comtrade data per year.

if all countries traded all products with all other countries, we would see around 275 million observations. [...] Thus, the roughly 9 million observed trade flows in recent years correspond to approximately 3.0% of the potential maximum number of observations.
Our recovered export values (Panel C) and import values (Panel D) are highly correlated with the IMF Balance of Payments (BoP) statistics across countries.
